questions.question: You depart on a cross-country flight at 0917 local time. Your flight is 128 nm on a true course of 028° and you will fly at 149 KTAS while burning 19.5 GPH. If the winds aloft are 170° at 23 kts, at what local time will the time to continue to the destination equal the time to return to the departure point?

Jack took off out of KOSH on his way to KJVL, on a course of 195° cruising across the ground at 72 KTS. Jill takes off in the opposite direction going from KJVL to KOSH on the 84.5 NM journey getting a ground speed of 110 KTS. (Wind is 220° at 21 KTS and both airplanes fly at 91 KTAS.) At what point will they pass each other?

You are departing New Orleans headed to Gulf Shores, which is a distance of 123 NM along a true course of 084°. Because you will be over water, you want to calculate your equal time point. If the winds are 330° at 22 knots and your true airspeed is 113 knots, how far from Gulf Shores will you be at your equal time point?
At this point, how long would it take you to fly back to New Orleans?
questions.context: Referenced question 23: You are departing New Orleans headed to Gulf Shores, which is a distance of 123 NM along a true course of 084°. questions.question: You are flying a 172 NM flight on a true course of 104° at a true airspeed of 127 knots. If the winds aloft are 230° at 17 knots, how long will you have been flying when the time to continue equals the time to return?
